title: Is Coachella Still Cool? 25 Years Later, Justin Bieber Reset Expectations | Vanity Fair
description: With Bieber fever rampant at this year’s festival, the answer might just be yes.
author: Daniela Tijerina
text: Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story Justin Bieber was only five years old when Coachella was founded in 1999. This year, he could make history as the festival’s highest-paid headliner since its inception. For a reported $10 million payday, Bieber fever swept through Indio during the Canadian singer’s 90-minute set on Saturday night, a divisive career retrospective that made the case for Coachella’s cultural relevance in 2026. Back in September, when the lineup for Coachella 2026 was announced, weekend passes sold out in just three days. (The lineup included fellow headliners Sabrina Carpenter and Karol G, the first-ever Latina headliner at Coachella.) While it didn’t sell out immediately, as it often did in a pre-pandemic world, ticket sales are still a strong indicator of renewed interest in experiencing the festival firsthand. Especially considering the costs incurred to attend, with general admission tickets for the first weekend starting at a whopping $649. Tack on travel and accommodations to that and Coachella becomes a major financial commitment. All weekend long I heard anecdotes about how the price of housing in the surrounding areas skyrocketed because of “Bieberchella,” with social media users claiming that some rental homes surged to an almost comical price tag just short of $100,000 . Some things come and go, but after what I witnessed this weekend, with a barricade drenched in the singer’s signature purple, Bieber fever is forever—and yes, speaking from experience, it’s contagious. Fans in front of the main stage cheer for The Strokes as they perform at Coachella on April 28, 2002. Sebastian Artz/Getty Images But this year’s edition of Coachella isn’t just for Beliebers. Among the 125,000 attendees who descend upon the desert each weekend are flocks of influencers sent by brands like Poppi, Gap, and Guess for an extravagant experience sure to leave their millions of followers envious while they watch on their phones from afar. Coachella has been deemed the “Influencer Olympics,” and attending the festival with a brand is still considered the ultimate status symbol on the internet, with invitations getting more and more cutthroat and the trips becoming increasingly over-the-top with each passing year. But recently it’s turned the grounds into a content farm, ultimately diminishing the festival’s cool factor. Try to make your way from one stage to another and you’re bombarded with brand activations, photo opportunities, and seemingly endless lines to purchase branded merchandise. If you didn’t get a photo in front of the Ferris wheel, were you even there? And it’s not just limited to the music festival itself anymore: An entire village pops up in the surrounding area, with influencers making the trek to events like Revolve Fest, Camp Poosh, and the 818 Outpost, gathering hoards of free products and capturing content along the way. I myself braved the heat to attend Hailey Bieber’s Rhode event, and as I attempted to venture back to the Coachella grounds in time to see Addison Rae, I witnessed groups of influencers battle it out for an Uber to the next event—completely deprioritizing the music, in a survival-of-the-fittest situation, piling into black cars like they were life boats. Of course, fashion is almost as synonymous with Coachella as the music is nowadays. But that’s also changed over the years, with It girls like Kylie Jenner and Hailey Bieber, who made elaborate bohemian outfits and flower crowns Pinterest-famous, opting for a more casual, effortless approach. They’ve traded in fringe and glitter for jeans and a T-shirt. Yet, as their desert style evolves, the throwback posts they share on Instagram still inspire the average festivalgoer to arrive in cosplay for a bygone era. Hailey Bieber, Kendall Jenner and Kylie Jenner at Coachella on April 10, 2015. Light Brigade/Bauer-Griffin/Getty Images Find your way back to the festival grounds at the Empire Polo Club, which spans hundreds of acres and features 160 acts performing throughout the weekend, and it’s easy to see why some attendees are willing to camp out, foregoing air conditioning and a real shower for 72 hours: for the experience. The festival’s curation, which gave me the option to see Katseye, Nine Inch Nails, David Byrne, and Young Thug all in the same weekend, is unlike any other offering in the festival landscape today. The best part about it is that the acts themselves seem genuinely excited to be there, making the festival a dream come true for artists and attendees alike. If you’re one of the lucky ones to obtain a coveted artist pass, you’ll see the inner workings of the festival—an impressive well-oiled machine, complete with decked-out golf carts whizzing through sandy paths carrying pop stars like precious cargo with entourages trailing behind. For a festival that can feel sanctioned into an elitist hierarchy, there’s a great equalizer in everyone having to endure the heat and dust together, from general admission to the artist’s compound: Even the stars have to use mobile bathrooms. It’s true that it’s a very different festival than it was 25 years ago. The inaugural festival hosted Beck, Rage Against the Machine, and Tool. Following the disastrous events of Woodstock ’99, it was a risk, and the first few years were a loss. Still, slowly but surely, Coachella gained both popularity and credibility, becoming a mainstay of alternative music. Then, in 2018, Beyoncé became the first Black woman to headline the festival and changed the game forever. In a historic performance, immortalized in the Netflix documentary Homecoming: A Film by Beyoncé, she paid stunning homage to HBCU traditions in a set that is widely—and correctly—viewed as one of the greatest performances of all time. Beck performs at the Coachella on May 1, 2004. Tim Mosenfelder/Getty Images While Bieber can’t compete with Beychella (sorry, nobody can), he did defy expectations on Saturday night by merely starting his set on time at 11:25 p.m. on the dot and immediately showcasing his silky smooth vocal abilities, performing a song off of his grammy-nominated album Swag. Appearing onstage in a pink hoodie and sunglasses—to a crowd that felt like it was comprised of the entire festival and included celebrities like Timothée Chalamet, Kylie and Kendall Jenner, Jacob Elordi, Teyana Taylor, Katy Perry, Justin Trudeau, Kim Kardashian, and Lewis Hamilton—a deafening roar traveled through the desert air. As Bieber made his way through a pared-back, introspective catalog of songs of his two most recent albums, some Beliebers, wearing cheeky but official merch stamped with “Future Mrs. Bieber” and “It’s not clocking to you,” were losing steam after camping out at the main stage for hours ahead of his performance. “He’s depressed,” one festivalgoer near me remarked. But when he sat down at a laptop and pulled up his old YouTube videos, the crowd went wild. Singing along to hits that he hasn’t performed in over a decade, like “Baby,” “Beauty and a Beat,” and “Never Say Never,” a woman in the crowd proclaimed, “We ain’t new to this, we true to this!” while another filmed herself sobbing in the audience. While some critics have called the headline set lazy and basically a giant advertisement for YouTube (who sponsors the festival livestream), there was something undeniably heartwarming about Bieber harmonizing with his younger self, in a full-circle moment. “No one will ever know even an ounce of what it’s taken to get here,” his wife, Hailey, wrote in an Instagram post after the performance. It was an endearing mix of nostalgia and evolution as the now father, who shouted out his wife and son, baby Jack, reclaimed his youth and appeared in good spirits as he made his way down memory lane. Admittedly some of the best acts of the weekend were the ones that also drew upon the past. New rock kid on the block, Sombr brought out Billy Corgan of the Smashing Pumpkins to sing his ’90s hit “1979”; Addison Rae channeled her innermost early aughts Blackout -era Britney; Sabrina Carpenter surprised Friday night festivalgoers with cameos from Susan Sarandon and Will Ferrell; and Y2K legends the Strokes stepped in as Bieber’s opening act on the main stage. But there’s really something for everyone just a hop, skip, and a jump away, with a spectrum of performances ranging from FKA Twigs, Iggy Pop, Dijon, and PinkPantheress to Major Lazer, Sexyy Red, and Turnstile. Yes, Coachella is most definitely the Influencer Olympics. Yes, it’s a 72-hour marathon—one filled with copious amounts of dust and port-a-potties. But like Bieber, if you can cut through all the noise, it’s possible to find your way back to the music. At Coachella, it’s certainly worth trying. title: Justin Bieber at Coachella review – pop’s troubled prince mostly hits right notes in low-energy set | Coachella | The Guardian
description: For a reportedly record-breaking amount of money, the increasingly reclusive star proves his voice is still golden in a headliner performance light on enthusiasm
author: https://www.theguardian.com/profile/adrian-horton
text: Justin Bieber performs at Coachella on Saturday night. Photograph: Kevin Mazur/Getty Images for Coachella View image in fullscreen Justin Bieber performs at Coachella on Saturday night. Photograph: Kevin Mazur/Getty Images for Coachella This article is more than 1 month old Review Justin Bieber at Coachella review – pop’s troubled prince mostly hits right notes in low-energy set This article is more than 1 month old Empire Polo Club, Indio, California For a reportedly record-breaking amount of money, the increasingly reclusive star proves his voice is still golden in a headliner performance light on enthusiasm T hroughout the Strokes’ main stage set on Saturday evening, you could see them: fans, many of them women, unaffected by the New York indie rockers as they pushed forward through the crowd to stake out spots hours in advance for the night’s closer, Justin Bieber . “I know why you’re here … JUSTIN BIEBER!” the Strokes’ Julian Casablancas joked, sort of, between songs. “We’re happy to lube you up for him.” Sabrina Carpenter at Coachella review – madcap maximalism from pop savant Read more Perhaps Casablancas picked up on an anxious energy from the crowd: the chance to see Bieber in a Coachella primetime slot seemed at once inevitable and improbable. Save a stripped-down Grammys performance and two very selective LA warm-up shows, the 32-year-old pop star had not performed publicly in over four years, since cancelling his 2022 Justice World Tour amid a host of health issues. Maybe it’s because vulnerability is an established element of a performer who, for years, appeared dead behind the eyes in public, or the fact that Bieber recently ditched the managerial framework that guided his rocky career, or the lingering sting of Frank Ocean’s disastrous headliner set in 2023, when a generationally beloved artist with little recent performance experience cracked under the pressure: few Coachella headliner sets have generated this much buzz – Saturday broke Coachella’s single-day ticket record – and perhaps this much parasocial concern. Thus there was palpable relief when Bieber showed up nearly on time for his 11.35pm red-eye slot, coyly shrouded in a pink hoodie and large shades but hitting every mellifluous note of All I Can Take, a relatively groovy track off his 2025 album Swag, followed by a rendition of Swag II standout Speed Demon so buttery I felt a frisson of joy. Bieber’s relative reclusiveness and seeming fragility, remarkable for one of the most-streamed artists in the world, is such that the sight of him alone on stage, surfing the gossamer synths of Swag’s career-rejuvenating production, triggers a protective instinct as well as wonder; nothing, not even a relatively self-indulgent run of all new tracks with no choreography and recessive set design, feels guaranteed. View image in fullscreen Photograph: Kevin Mazur/Getty Images for Coachella He must know it counts for a lot, for this comeback set, a real test if there has ever been one for a very famous person’s live credibility, banked near entirely on Bieber’s slyly formidable instrument. If, just 24 hours earlier, Sabrina Carpenter made good on the headliner promise with a wildly ambitious, maximalist extravaganza , Bieber presented its foil: a minimalist set with just the singer, his mic and his laptop livestream, the cavernous main stage turned into his metaphorical Swag-era bedroom. Depending on your level of fandom, the stripped-down vision, with minimal audience asides, read as either radically vulnerable or disappointingly self-interested from reportedly the highest-paid Coachella headliner of all time. (The double standard for effort for female and male pop headliners is … striking.) Haloed by prismatic videography and cocooned by a singular gray mound that appeared a bit like a skate park (or perhaps like his wife Hailey’s viral lip-gloss phone case), Bieber performed the most modest of concert stripteases – over the course of his 90-minute set, he shed the shades and the hood but little of his hard-earned armor, saving a full look into his eyes for near halfway through. Few pop stars can make a single smile, flashed almost entirely for the new collaborators who joined him on stage, feel like a victory. As one of the world’s most famous late millennials, a generational icon who bled on the edge of both monocultural and viral fame, Bieber commands, from some, an immense well of generous goodwill. His abject plaintiveness for the Prince-inflected Go Baby – sat on the ground, elbows on knees and eyes closed – managed to briefly make the 100,000-person experience feel small, exposed, as if asking the crowd to take him as himself. But audience momentum is a fickle thing; the Swag double albums have their handful of genuine highlights, but 11 clipped, gauzy, bedroom R&B-style tracks is a heavy ask to open a comeback set. Bieber, an instinctive and compelling performer despite it all, seemed to sense this – “I think you guys need some energy,” he said before launching into the propulsive track Stay with The Kid Laroi that jolted the crowd back to life and reminded just how much ache Bieber’s voice can conjure even in a compressed feature. But he immediately flat-lined again with a six-song Swag acoustic moment that, while beautifully sung, leaned too heavily into his preacher’s instinct; I too love the music festival, but not even Justin Bieber is going to convince me to chant: “Coachella, Hallelujah.” The xx at Coachella review – indie trio reunites for spellbinding, rangy set Read more View image in fullscreen Photograph: Kevin Mazur/Getty Images for Coachella The highlight was always going to be how Bieber grappled with the past that so clearly still weighs on the present, the painful public growth that still colors his stunning voice (if this set proved one thing, it’s that I would listen to him sing the phone book, though I will always hope for better). Evincing a sense of humor we so rarely get to see, Bieber took the crowd down a YouTube rabbit hole, searching on his laptop for the music videos for such hits as Sorry, breakout single Baby (!), Beauty and the Beat (!!) and All That Matters (!!!). To palpable audience shock, he harmonized along with his younger, less ripened voice, and showed clips of him running into a glass door as a mega-famous child and, for some reason, the Deez Nuts video. The effect was both frustrating – would it really kill him to do more than one verse of 2015 anthem Where Are U Now? – and endearing; many tears were shed to the image of the man singing back to his 13-year-old self, a precocious child busking in Ontario to Neyo’s So Sick. The nostalgia wormhole perhaps closed a loop, though it did not scratch the itch for a more comprehensive career assessment. The past, it seems, is still an uncomfortable coat to be worn, though at least now with some cheek. Bieber seemed much more at home back in the Swag cycle and with a stable of collaborators – Dijon (for Devotion), Tems (I Think You’re Special), Wizkid (Essence) and Mk.gee, all of whom seemed to be doing Bieber a favor, anointing him as a genuine creative with artistic vision, than vice versa. The latter lent his guitar fireworks to a blooming finale of Daisies, Swag’s most radio-friendly and least snoozy track, appropriately accompanied by real fireworks. Note-perfect to the finish, Bieber appeared triumphant – it might not have been all that we wanted to hear, but for better and for worse, he said what he needed to say. title: Watch Justin Bieber Bring Out SZA and Billie Eilish at Coachella | Pitchfork
description: Sexyy Red, Dijon, and Big Sean also joined Bieber onstage for his Weekend Two set
author: Hattie Lindert
text: Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story Justin Bieber once again headlined Coachella on Saturday, April 20, and brought a host of special guests with him. He sang his “Snooze” remix with SZA , his Swag songs “Sweet Spot” with Sexyy Red and “Devotion” with Dijon , and “As Long As You Love Me” and “No Pressure” with Big Sean . He also brought up dedicated Belieber Billie Eilish during a performance of “One Less Lonely Girl,” reigniting a tradition that dates back to his early touring days. Watch footage from the occasion below. At his set last weekend , Bieber brought out Tems, Wizkid, the Kid Laroi, Mk.Gee, and Dijon. His Weekend Two show followed the same format as Weekend One’s, opening with a number of new songs before Bieber busted out a laptop to revisit some of his biggest early-career hits. New additions this time around included “U Smile,” his first big hit, “One Time,” and a cover of Justin Timberlake’s “Cry Me a River” (“I’m proud of you—and you should be proud of you too,” Timberlake said on Instagram ). He closed the night out with “Daisies.” Bieber released Swag in July 2025, following it up with a sequel in September. Swag earned him nominations at the 2026 Grammys for Album of the Year and Best Pop Vocal Album, as well as Best Pop Solo Performance for “Daisies” and Best R&B Performance for “Yukon.” Read more about Swag at No. 13 in The 30 Best Pop Albums of 2025 . title: Watch Justin Bieber’s Coachella 2026 Performance - Setlist Details and Celebs in the Crowd
description: After months of preparation, Justin Bieber returned to the stage at Coachella, performing his first-week headlining show. Here, details about the 2026 performance.
text: THE RUNDOWN Justin Bieber returned to Coachella to perform his headlining set. The star stuck to songs from his new albums Swag and Swag II , performing songs like “Yukon” and “Daisies.” Hailey Bieber and Kylie Jenner were spotted in the audience, supporting him. After months of preparation, Justin Bieber returned to the stage at Coachella, performing his first headlining show at the festival last night. Bieber gave an intimate performance, pulling from his albums Swag and Swag II for his setlist. Standout moments during the set included Bieber revisiting his past works with a live search through YouTube and a guest appearance from The Kid Laroi. Fans quickly shared footage from his show on social media: View full post on X View full post on X View full post on X View full post on X His wife, Hailey Bieber, was spotted in the crowd with her friend, Kylie Jenner. View full post on X The full set list included songs from his most recent albums and his past work: View full post on X Back in October, a source detailed to People how much effort Bieber was putting into his Coachella performance. At that point, Bieber was on Twitch, showing how he and his team were preparing for the festival. A source close to him told the outlet that Bieber was purposely showcasing his process as “his way of letting fans see that he’s excited and working hard for his headlining set at Coachella.” In September, shortly after Bieber was announced as a headliner for the festival, a source gave People an update on how he and his wife were doing amid the news. “Both Justin and Hailey are thriving right now,” the source said. “They just celebrated their wedding anniversary; he’s on top of the charts; and she’s breaking records in beauty. They are both very focused. Justin’s super grateful for his fans. He’s hyped about Coachella. Hailey will be there of course.” Bieber recently performed “Yukon” at the Grammys, marking one of his first onstage appearances in years. TMZ reported that last weekend, he had a private concert as well at the Troubadour in L.A.

title: Where To Buy Justin Bieber's SKYLRK x SharkNinja Collab ChillPill Fan
description: Justin Bieber's week one 2026 Coachella set was great, but the heat was not. Here's how I survived out in the desert with Bieber's new SharkNinja fan.
author: Amina Ayoud
text: By Amina Ayoud Amina Ayoud Contact Amina Ayoud by Email View all posts by Amina Ayoud April 15, 2026 Kevin Mazur/Getty Images for Coachella/SharkNinja If you purchase an independently reviewed product or service through a link on our website, Rolling Stone may receive an affiliate commission. I was one of the lucky few to see Justin Bieber this past weekend at Coachella 2026. The almost two-hour-long romp had me standing, singing, dancing, and sometimes crying with throngs of Beliebers. The set was a nostalgic one, featuring old tracks like the famed hit “Baby,” “Beauty and a Beat,” and “Never Say Never,” along with newer songs like “Daisies” and “I Think You’re Special” off of his 2025 album Swag . The set was also chock-full of cameos from fellow artists like Tems, Wizkid, Dijon, The Kid Laroi, and Mk.gee. While I enjoyed every minute, the heat from all those dancing Beliebers was pretty unbearable. The only reason a sweaty girl like me got through the weekend was because of Shark’s ChillPill Personal Fan , a handheld piece of tech that was a godsend. It just so happens that Bieber dropped a special color Shark ChillPill fan in collaboration with his fashion and accessories brand SKYLRK just in time for Coachella weekend two. Shark ChillPill Personal Fan SKYLRK-Exclusive $150 Buy Now at Shark Ninja This bright orange handheld Shark ChillPill fan was made in collaboration with Justin Bieber. The fan comes with two alternative heads. The tech currently retails for $150 on SharkNinja’s website and comes in a vibrant orange hue. The desert weekend one was warm and sunny, with some days nearing highs of 90°F. Safe to say, I needed this fan desperately. The ChillPill was with me at all times, nestled in my back pocket or in my crossbody bag, keeping me cool with controlled blasts of cold air that you can adjust as you see fit with an intuitive dial touchscreen. I didn’t have the pleasure of testing out the SKYLRK-inspired one, but the functionality is the same. Related: Where to Buy Justin Bieber Merch Online The dial goes from one to 10, controlling the strength of the airflow. To turn the fan on, you’ll flip a silver button on the side up before pressing and holding the touch screen. The fan lasted hours, through multiple sets, without needing a charge. Shark claims the fan can last for up to 11 hours, which sounds about right considering how long I was on the festival grounds each day. The ChillPill comes with two interchangeable heads that click into place, offering unique functions that the fan head can’t provide. My personal favorite was the InstaChill cooling plate, a cooling metal plate that feels heavenly when pressed against the neck or forehead. Shark says that this cooling plate is said to lower your skin’s temperature by up to 16°F. There’s also a dry-touch mister that can be opened and filled up before use. When the fan or InstaChill didn’t cut it, this mister saved me on some of the hottest days. Bonus: this feature didn’t ruin my makeup. In fact, it kind of gave me a hydrated look that I appreciated out in the dry and dusty desert. I used this fan all three blazing hot days at Coachella, and I am pretty sure I survived because of it. Pick up the Bieber-color ChillPill while it’s still in stock at SharkNinja.com now .

title: At Coachella, Justin Bieber spends half an hour surfing YouTube
description: Justin Bieber, a Coachella 2026 headliner, played few hits and often watched YouTube videos on stage in Indio, California.
author: Timothy Karoff
text: INDIO, Calif. — This was not the Justin Bieber the world remembered. It was probably not the Justin Bieber they wanted, either. Each year, Coachella has three headliners . But unofficially, the Canadian popstar was the Indio festival’s main act. It seems hardly a coincidence that 2026, the year of Bieber’s return, was the first year that Coachella had quickly sold out since 2022. This was “Bieberchella.” Three years after retreating from the spotlight, his Saturday night set was set to be his biggest performance in years. What fans hoped for, it seems, was vintage Bieber. They wanted the hits: “Baby,” “One Less Lonely Girl,” “Sorry.” They wanted nostalgia; they wanted a dance party. They wanted a pop star with a microphone headset and a flock of backup dancers. They wanted choreography. Advertisement Article continues below this ad Bieber had other ideas. Saturday’s 90-minute performance was stubbornly underproduced, so lo-fi that it felt like an overcorrection from his polished career as a teenage star. The singer appeared on stage alone, with a pink hoodie pulled over his head and sunglasses across his eyes. He selected his backing tracks manually from a Mac on the side of the stage. Occasionally, he scrolled through comments on the Coachella livestream to pick his next songs. “I was thinking maybe you guys could help me with the songs tonight, maybe help pick the favorites,” he explained, sharing the livestream’s comments to the screens on the stage. Those favorites apparently excluded his old material. The singer played only snippets of “Baby,” “Never Say Never,” “Beauty and a Beat” and his other early hits. Instead, Bieber pulled mostly from his newest albums, 2025’s “Swag” and “Swag II.” The albums are two discs of stripped-back, lo-fi R&B, a departure from his pop sound. The only other songs he played in full weren’t his own: “Stay” (which Bieber does feature on), with special guest The Kid Laroi; and “Essence,” with Wizkid and Tems. Advertisement Article continues below this ad It’s clear that this is how Bieber likes it. Some of the “Swag” tracks sounded beautiful live. Bieber’s opener, “All I Can Take,” was an immediate reminder of his voice’s power. (With sunglasses covering his eyes and a hood over his head, there was nothing else to focus on aside from his voice, anyway.) Things got weird a third of the way through the set when Bieber started playing through his old material. Or rather, his computer did. The singer sat perched in front of his Mac, searching for his old music videos on YouTube. He shared his computer’s screen so that the crowd could see. When he clicked on the video for “Baby,” the audience’s reaction was immediate. What had been a quiet crowd jumped to attention. Phones flew up, and fans sang along. Bieber did too, quietly, his new voice an octave lower. And then, after a minute, he pulled the plug. He searched for another song, “Favorite Girl,” and did the same routine: one minute, then on to the next. And so on for “Confident,” “All That Matters” and “Sorry,” among others. Advertisement Article continues below this ad More Coachella — The best and worst of Coachella 2026 — Karol G makes history with Coachella headlining set — At Coachella, Justin Bieber spends half an hour surfing YouTube — Nine Inch Noize's Coachella set may go down as one of the festival's best ever “Do you really go all the way back?” Bieber kept saying as he searched for the next song. “Like for real for real?” For Bieber, this segment of his set seemed to be less a performance than a trip down memory lane. On some tracks, he let his younger self do the heavy lifting, singing along on every other line. “Sorry to cut it, but these are just little snippets,” he said at another point. “I wanna see how far back you really go.” Advertisement Article continues below this ad For good measure, he surfed around on YouTube for longer, cycling through some viral videos. Among them, the double rainbow video, Deez Nuts and a clip of his viral “it’s not clocking to you” confrontation with the paparazzi. Bieber has lived a whole lifetime — or several — since he was first plucked from YouTube obscurity. From the days that he sang on the Ellen show or wore a purple hoodie , Bieber has grown up in the spotlight, and stepped into adulthood under the close scrutiny of paparazzi, tabloids and millions of fans. In time, his voice dropped. He got a DUI, covered his body in tattoos, hard-launched as a Christian and left behind his younger image to start singing about sex . He battled substance abuse . He got married and had a son. He transitioned from a teen idol with legions of obsessive fans to an ordinary pop star. Justin Bieber and Tems perform at the Coachella Stage. Kevin Mazur/Getty Images for Coachella Saturday’s performance was set to be something of a comeback for Bieber. The artist cancelled the remainder of his last tour , the Justice World Tour, in 2023. The cancellation came after Bieber postponed earlier shows in the tour over an unspecified illness, and announced that he had Ramsay Hunt syndrome , a neurological condition that had partially paralyzed his face. The cancellation cost him, according to People : The company of his former manager, Scooter Braun, covered the cost of the cancellation, and as part of a settlement, Bieber reportedly paid Braun back $26 million last year. Advertisement Article continues below this ad In the years since, the singer has kept quiet. He released no music himself for three years. He sold his entire catalog for $200 million as rumors circulated that his finances were tenuous and that he had replaced his old team with a circle of yes-men . He split with Braun , who had managed him since 2008. In the summer of 2025, Bieber stepped back into the spotlight. “Swag” and “Swag II” are his first standalone projects since 2021’s “Justice.” The albums are produced in part by Dijon, who was a special guest on Saturday. In February, Bieber performed a song shirtless at the Grammys. Considering the evidence presented at Saturday’s Coachella performance — the denied gratification, the hits cut short — one could draw the conclusion that Justin Bieber is trolling his fans. But hearing him sing passionately through “Yukon,” and watching him smile while flipping through his old music videos, it seems that this is just the thing that makes him happy. Justin Bieber wanted an intimate set, something simple to connect him to his fans and his past. Advertisement Article continues below this ad For some, “intimate” might be a synonym for “boring.” But at least one person had a great time: “I hope you guys drive home safe,” Bieber said, almost timidly, before signing off. “Drink some water. I loved spending this time with you guys.” More music — He was blamed for pop music's demise but got a hero's welcome at Coachella — 'We're f—ked': California's music festival bubble is bursting — The 1990s Bay Area beach rave that collectively hallucinated a UFO — I found the Bay Area dock where Otis Redding wrote his final song Justin Bieber, draped in black, waves to the crowd from the Coachella Stage. title: Watch Justin Bieber's Coachella 2026 Performance
description: Justin Bieber brings 'SWAG,' throwback hits, memes, and more to the Coachella stage. Here's what to know.
text: Music Justin Bieber's Coachella Performance: 'SWAG,' Throwback Hits, Memes, and More Bieber follows his unforgettable Grammys performance with a headlining Coachella set. By Trace William Cowen April 12, 2026 Share Image via Kevin Mazur/Getty Images for Coachella Justin Bieber had the eyes of the world on him on Saturday (April 11) as he brought SWAG and more from his catalog to the Coachella stage. These days, as fans know, getting a full-scale Bieber performance has become a rarity. His most recent outing, the Justice World Tour, initially faced pandemic-related delays before launching in early 2022. The tour was ultimately cut short , however, with Bieber citing his health. In February of this year, Bieber delivered one of the strongest performances of the night as part of the 2026 Grammys ceremony . For the performance, Bieber, whose SWAG was up for Album of the Year, commanded an intimately delivered take on “Yukon,” a highlight from the album. For Saturday’s Coachella set, Bieber played through his catalog with a focus on his latest album, SWAG . He also brought out some surprises, including bringing out The Kid LAROI for “Stay” and even detouring into some of his old hits that he pulled up on a laptop via YouTube. He played his breakout hit “Baby,” “Favorite Girl” for the first time since 2013, “Beauty and the Beat” featuring Nicki Minaj, and “Never Say Never.” Notably, Bieber sang along to his cover of “So Sick” by Ne-Yo, the first of many covers uploaded to YouTube starting in 2007 that eventually led to him getting discovered and signed. Bieber also pulled up his infamous paparazzi video where he said, “Is it not clocking to you?” before transitioning back into the music, singing along to “I’m the One.” He appeared to get sidetracked, pulling up old memes such as “Gottem” and the classic “Double Rainbow” video. He also brought out guests Dijon, Tems, Wizkid and Mk.gee to close out his set. Other performers include Addison Rae, Young Thug , Ethel Cain, Clipse , Sexyy Red , Slayyyter, Geese, Die Spitz, PinkPantheress , Central Cee, The Strokes, and more. Dijon , a key Bieber collaborator whose excellent Baby album arrived last August , is also on the lineup. title: All the Details From Justin Bieber’s 2026 Coachella Headlining Performance—and How to Watch
description: On weekend one of the music and arts festival, the pop singer treated fans to intimate set that included performances of everything from “Baby” to “Daisies.” Get all the details, and find out how to watch videos from set, here.
text: Kevin Mazur // Getty Images Saturday night was one for the Beliebers during the first weekend of the 2026 Coachella Valley Music and Arts Festival . Late on April 11, Justin Bieber held his first-ever headlining gig at the festival in Indio, California, where he wove through a medley of hits, including some old and new, for a sizable crowd that reportedly extended all the way back to the iconic ferris wheel on the sprawling festival grounds. To kick things off, Bieber crooned a number of songs from his 2025 sister albums, Swag and Swag II , including tracks like “All I Can Take,” “Butterflies,” “Speed Demon,” and “First Place.” Kevin Mazur // Getty Images Justin Bieber performs on weekend one of the Coachella music festival. Then, the pop singer turned his set into a makeshift “ music video night ,” pulling up YouTube on a laptop and singing along to fan favorites from some of his earliest albums, including “Baby,” “Favorite Girl,” and “That Should Be Me.” He even searched clips from before his ascent to stardom, performing alongside videos of himself as a tween, singing songs like Ne-Yo’s “So Sick” and “With You” by Chris Brown. (He wasn’t afraid to poke a little fun at himself either, playing clips that saw him falling on stage at a concert and running into a glass door.) During the set, the Grammy winner brought out a few surprise guests, including Dijon, Tems, Mk.gee, Wizkid, and the Kid Laroi. In the audience, his wife, Hailey , danced alongside fans in the crowd. If you’re looking to check out clips from the performance, the official Coachella YouTube account typically uploads videos around 24 hours after the set has taken place (keep checking back here for more updates as videos arrive). In the meantime, you can watch various clips from the performance below. title: Justin Bieber Serenades an Ecstatic Billie Eilish at Coachella
description: Justin Bieber brought Billie Eilish onstage to serenade her during weekend two of Coachella.
text: THE RUNDOWN Justin Bieber performed his second headlining set at Coachella. While singing his 2009 hit “One Less Lonely Girl,” he invited Billie Eilish onstage. The “Birds of a Feather” singer was completely thrilled to be sung to by Bieber. On Saturday night, Justin Bieber performed for his second weekend at Coachella in Indio, California, welcoming a number of special guests onstage. But one of the most excited guests was pop star Billie Eilish . Eilish didn’t perform with Bieber. Instead, she got to play the part of a superfan. Eilish has been very open about her love of Bieber’s music for years. The “Baby” singer traditionally serenades a fan from the audience while performing his 2009 hit “One Less Lonely Girl.” Eilish was encouraged to go up on the stage to take on the role, falling down on her knees repeatedly in excitement. View full post on X Bieber encouraged her to get up, hugging her from behind as he continued to sing. As he finished, he told her, “Only you, shawty.” They hugged before Eilish headed back into the crowd. Eilish has met Bieber before and they even worked together on the remix to her song “Bad Guy.” But this was clearly a very different experience for the 24-year-old singer. View full post on X During his set, Bieber also welcomed rappers Big Sean and Sexyy Red, as well as SZA. During his first performance at Coachella on April 11, he performed songs from his latest albums SWAG and SWAG II . That night, he brought up The Kid LAROI, Dijon, Tems, WizKid, and Mk.gee. The show was attended last week by Kylie Jenner, Timothée Chalamet, Jacob Elordi, and Hailey Bieber, who was back in the audience this week. The Rhode founder supposedly pushed Eilish onto the stage for her big moment, encouraging her to join her hero onstage. View full post on X

title: Justin Bieber Returns With Coachella Headlining Set
description: Justin Bieber played his biggest concert in years as he headlined Coachella on Saturday, delivering a stripped back set that leaned heavy on "Swag.
author: Ethan Millman
text: Justin Bieber performs at Coachella. Kevin Mazur/Getty Images for Coachella Share on Facebook Share on X Google Preferred Share to Flipboard Show additional share options Share on LinkedIn Share on Pinterest Share on Reddit Share on Tumblr Share on Whats App Send an Email Print the Article Post a Comment Justin Bieber played his biggest concert in years as he headlined Coachella on Saturday night, delivering an intimate concert that leaned heavy on his Swag era and had Bieber mining through old YouTube clips on his laptop to take fans back to the early days. Bieber’s show was stripped back, particularly compared to Sabrina Carpenter ’s grandiose headlining show from the day prior. The pop star was mainly on his own for most of the set as he crooned and paced around a halfpipe-looking structure that took up the bulk of the stage. There were no backup dancers, no intricate stage props, and no outfit or stage design changes. The only times anyone else took the stage was when a couple guitarists came out for a few tracks, and for a few special guests (Tems, Wizkid, Dijon and The Kid Laroi) to join. It appeared Bieber wanted to keep the focus squarely on him and his voice. Related Stories Music Scooter Braun on Spencer Pratt: "He's Speaking for a Lot of People Who Are Very Frustrated" Music BTS Sweeps American Music Awards as Sombr Wins Two Televised Trophies Justin Bieber brings out The Kid Laroi for “Stay” during his #Coachella set pic.twitter.com/OS1jlFSJbA — The Hollywood Reporter (@THR) April 12, 2026 “Wow wow wow, to be up close and personal with you guys, this is special,” a hooded Bieber told the Coachella crowd Saturday. “This is a night I dreamed about for a long time, so to be here is amazing.” Bieber’s return follows a period of immense change for one of the world’s biggest pop acts over the past several years. He’d canceled tour dates over health issues, sold his publishing catalog, became a father and parted ways with longtime manager Scooter Braun. He came back with his first album in four years last year with Swag and its deluxe Swag II, earning a Grammy nomination for album of the year. Kicking off at about 11:30 p.m., Bieber spent much of the first chunk of his set focused around his Swag era, opening with “All I Can Take” before going into “Speed Demon,” “First Place,” and “Go Baby.” He stuck with Swag songs until he brought out his first surprise guest of the evening, with The Kid Laroi coming out to perform their 2021 smash hit “Stay.” By the middle of his set, the show was like a karaoke party, with Bieber pulling up YouTube links of his childhood hits, taking his own trip down memory lane and testing the crowd to see how far back they can go. “Tonight is such a special night, but I feel like we’ve gotta take you guys on a bit of a journey,” Bieber said as he was fiddling around on a laptop hooked up to the large screens behind him. He started a YouTube search that sent the crowd into a brief frenzy as they realized he was typing in “Baby.” “You guys remember this song?” he asked. It’s not Justin Bieber without “Baby” 💜🥹🎡 #Coachella pic.twitter.com/bmW65TXMZy — The Hollywood Reporter (@THR) April 12, 2026 From there, he started singing quick snippets alongside the recordings of his oldest classics, which also included “Beauty and a Beat,” “Never Say Never,” and “Confident.” Seeing a baby-faced Bieber on screen at Coachella was a stark reminder of just how long he’s been famous, and while YouTube karaoke felt low-effort compared to a more involved production, it did make for a compelling visual to see the fully grown Bieber sing these songs as his childhood played in the rear view. Still, as it went on it became more confounding than profound, as tracks like “Sorry” may have been better performed in a more official capacity rather than through an improvised music video singalong. “Wi-Fi, come on man,” Bieber said as the crowd chuckled when the “Sorry” video buffered as he pulled it up. Eventually the bit turned to millennial-era memes, as Bieber pulled up the “Deez Nuts” and “Double Rainbow” YouTube videos, with Bieber saying he was “getting pulled into the deep dark web, we’ve got to keep the show going.” He then abandoned the laptop and started singing “Yukon.” Dijon, who played the festival yesterday, joined in for “Devotion,” after, and Tems came out next to duet with Bieber on “I Think You’re Special. Wizkid joined after to perform a cover of his song “Essense.” Bieber finished the set with “Daisies,” with guitarist Mk.gee, who co-produced the song, coming out for the finale. Fireworks went off behind Bieber as he told the crowd he loved them and then quickly ran off stage. He’s got one more headlining set next Saturday. title: Justin Bieber, Sabrina Carpenter’s Albums Rocket Up Charts After Coachella
description: Bieber's ‘SWAG’ and Carpenter's ‘Man's Best Friend’ zoomed up the charts thanks to their headlining performances during Coachella's first weekend.
text: Music Justin Bieber and Sabrina Carpenter’s Albums Rocket Up Billboard Charts After Coachella Sets Bieber's ‘SWAG’ and Carpenter's ‘Man's Best Friend’ zoomed up the charts thanks to their headlining performances during Coachella's first weekend. By Alex Ocho April 19, 2026 Share Images via Kevin Mazur/Getty Images for Coachella; Frazer Harrison/Getty Images Justin Bieber and Sabrina Carpenter's albums surged back up the Billboard charts following their headlining performances at Coachella . According to Billboard , Bieber’s SWAG vaults from No. 55 to No. 7 with 43,000 equivalent album units, driven largely by streaming gains after he headlined the second night of Coachella’s first weekend last Saturday (April 11). Carpenter's Man's Best Friend also returns to the top 10, rising from No. 18 to No. 10 with 40,000 units after her headlining performance last Friday (April 10), with streaming activity accounting for most of the increase. Elsewhere, Ella Langley earns her first No. 1 on the Billboard 200 as Dandelion debuts atop the chart with 169,000 equivalent album units. The release, featuring the hit “Choosin’ Texas,” marks the largest week for a country album by a woman in two years and the biggest week for any woman in 2026. Morgan Wallen's I'm the Problem holds at No. 2 with 83,000 units, BTS' ARIRANG falls from No. 1 to No. 3 in its fourth week with 78,000 units after a three-week run at the top. Don Toliver's OCTANE remains at No. 4 with 48,000 units, Olivia Dean's The Art of Loving stays at No. 5 with nearly 48,000 units, and Bad Bunny's Debí Tirar Más Fotos rises from No. 7 to No. 6 with 44,000 units. Luke Combs' The Way I Am drops from No. 6 to No. 8 with 42,000 units, while Morgan Wallen's One Thing at a Time climbs from No. 9 to No. 8 with 40,000 units. For Bieber’s second Coachella set (April 18), Bieber brought out Billie Eilish to dedicate “One Less Lonely Girl” to her. His set also saw surprise guests Sexyy Red to perform “Sweet Spot,” Big Sean for “As Long as You Love Me” and “No Pressure,” and SZA for “Snooze.” Meanwhile, Sabrina Carpenter’s second Coachella set featured appearances from Geena Davis, Terry Crews, and Madonna , who performed an “Vogue,” an unreleased song seemingly called “Bring Your Love,” and “Like a Prayer.” Related Stories music Justin Bieber Sings "One Less Lonely Girl" to Billie Eilish at Coachella Eilish was seemingly shocked and overwhelmed by the experience. title: Justin Bieber's Streams Surge After Coachella Headline Set
description: Justin Bieber enjoyed his biggest streaming day of the year, as well as huge boosts on Spotify, after headlining Coachella weekend one.
author: Jon Blistein
text: By Jon Blistein Jon Blistein Contact Jon Blistein by Email View all posts by Jon Blistein April 14, 2026 Justin Bieber performing at this year’s Coachella Kevin Mazur/Getty Images Justin Bieber ‘s highly-anticipated return to the stage has unsurprisingly led to a spike in streams for the pop star. According to preliminary data from Luminate, Bieber’s music was streamed 24.6 million times in the U.S. on April 12, the day after he headlined Coachella . That was a 54 percent jump from his total streams on April 11 (15.9 million), and a 74 percent leap from April 10 (14.1 million). The 24.6 million streams Bieber enjoyed on April 12 also made it his biggest streaming day of the year so far, and largest since July 18, 2025, one week after he released Swag . Spotify, meanwhile, shared data with Rolling Stone showing that Bieber’s music enjoyed a 250 percent increase in streams globally on that platform since Coachella . While that’s boosted songs across Bieber’s discography, his Coachella set — which featured his two Swag records alongside old favorites — appears to have really benefited his back catalog. For instance, Bieber’s 2010 hit “U Smile” jumped a whopping 600 percent versus its average totals; 2012’s “One Love” rose by 550 percent; and “Favorite Girl” — which Bieber hasn’t performed live in over a decade — surged by 310 percent. Other 2010s classics enjoying a boost were “Never Say Never” (320 percent), “As Long As You Love Me” (340 percent), and “Baby” (170 percent, though maybe Geese can take some partial credit for that). But fans aren’t ignoring Bieber’s newer material either. Streams of “I Do” off Swag II rose by 435 percent, while “All I Can Take” and “Walking Away,” both off Swag , saw boosts of 320 and 310 percent, respectively. Additionally, “I Think You’re Special” featuring Tems, and “Devotion” featuring Dijon, rose by 280 percent and 240 percent, respectively; both Tems and Dijon joined Bieber for performances of those songs at Coachella. Trending Stories Jay-Z and Eminem to Reunite on Rakim Album With First Track Together in 25 Years Late-Night Hosts Take Aim at Trump’s Knicks Game Appearance: ‘He Hit Them With a Jinx’ Taylor Swift and Randy Newman Perform Duet of ‘You’ve Got a Friend in Me’ at ‘Toy Story 5’ Premiere Watch Rush Play All of ‘2112’ for the First Time in 29 Years Bieber isn’t the only artist to see a streaming boost from Coachella weekend one, with his fellow headliner Karol G also enjoying Spotify surges . Karol’s global streams jumped 14.3 percent, while in the U.S., her streams spiked more than double that with 35 percent. For Bieber, his Coachella set marked his first major public performance since his Justice tour ended in 2022. (Bieber postponed, then canceled the run while dealing with a series of health issues.) Since then, he’s been largely absent from the stage, outside a handful of private gigs and TV performances. Ahead of Coachella, he took the stage at the Roxy in Los Angeles for an invite-only event for fans .

title: Justin Bieber Coachella Debate Rage: Pop Shop Podcast
description: Justin Bieber's Coachella headlining set might be getting mixed reviews across social media, but one thing is for sure: People are talking.
author: Katie Atkinson,Keith Caulfield
text: Justin Bieber performs at the Coachella Stage during the 2026 Coachella Valley Music and Arts Festival at Empire Polo Club on April 11, 2026 in Indio, California. Kevin Mazur/Getty Images for Coachella Trending on Billboard Justin Bieber ‘s Saturday night headlining set at Coachella might be getting mixed reviews across social media at the moment, but one thing is for sure: People are definitely talking about it. title: Justin Bieber's Coachella-Stage YouTube Deep Dive Was Thrilling and Cathartic | GQ
description: You're going to have to give him a minute—Justin Bieber has to think about his entire life before he plays “Daisies.”
author: Molly Mary O'Brien
text: Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story What do you think of when you hear the phrase “Justin Bieber concert”? Do you imagine royal purple confetti and a mop of hair swooshing to and fro? Perhaps an army of exuberant dancers, commanded by a teenager in harem pants? Do you imagine, rather than a completed concert, the spectre of a canceled one, as was the case with many of Bieber’s Justice Tour shows back in 2022? Push these images to the back of your memory closet, and replace them with Justin Bieber’s 2026 Coachella set: a gently swirling light show, a laptop on a stand, and a be-hoodied singer in a pair of sturdy galoshes doing delicate laps of the stage. The Canadian singer-songwriter dispensed with the teenybopper-stimulating pomp of his earlier days on last year's SWAG and SWAG II , largely stripped-down ventures into lo-fi bedroom pop and warm, retro R&B; his Grammy performance in February saw him looping the simple guitar part to his hit song “YUKON” in just shorts and socks, his voice as elastic and angelic as it’s ever been. And Bieber’s Coachella performance was similarly austere—34 songs, very little embellishment, and a very strong confidence in the bond between the singer and his true Beliebers to pull everything off. At a time when Coachella virality often begins with elaborate stage production—just look at Friday headliner Sabrina Carpenter, who staged a complex Old Hollywood-themed drama complete with Fosse choreography and backup dancers dressed like toy poodles—going minimal was a bold choice for Bieber. Performing atop a curvaceously molded stage set to what sounded like a pre-recorded backing track, with no stage band visible, he opened his set with “ALL I CAN TAKE,” the first of many SWAG songs on deck. Unique camera angles abounded: one on the floor that Bieber leaned over to sing into, as if pondering his reflection in a well; another on the laptop he occasionally visited, where he encouraged the stream’s live chat to make requests. When collaborators Carter Lang and Dylan Wiggins came out with acoustic guitars to accompany Bieber, he was popping SWAG songs out—”THINGS YOU DO”, “GLORY VOICE MEMO”, “ZUMA HOUSE”—like t-shirts fired from a cannon. But these were chill ballads, and it did not seem to be shaping up to be a hype Saturday night set. The energy briefly spiked when The Kid LAROI stepped up on the stage monolith to sing “Stay,” but by the time Bieber shouted out his wife Hailey Bieber in the crowd on “EVERYTHING HALLELUJAH” (“Hailey, baby, hallelujah”), the mood was almost punishingly mellow. Then something weird and sublime happened to shake things up—Bieber started pulling up his old YouTube videos on the laptop and singing along. I don’t think I’ve ever seen an artist channel the late-night behavior of nostalgic digital natives (or maybe the behavior exhibited at Gay Guy Music Video Night ) into concert format, and watching it live was a real trip. Bieber sang along to the ancient Usher and Chris Brown covers that first garnered the attention of Scooter Braun, pitching his voice an octave lower than that of his cherubic former self. Along with the audiences in Indio and at home, he watched the video for “Baby,” looking perhaps a tad wistful as clips of 14-year-old Baby Bieber cavorting adorably at a bowling alley were projected on the screen behind him. The audience, thirsty for the biggest hits in the catalog, lost it when he cued up “Sorry” and “Where Are U Now.” Then, like anyone who spends too long on YouTube, he got lost in the sauce—he pulled up footage of him running into a glass door in Germany in 2010 , then the video of him accosting paparazzi last year in Malibu with the now famous “It’s not clocking to you that I’m standing on business” line. Completely in the weeds of a viral video spiral, he played the original Deez Nuts video, as well as the video of the guy yelling about the double rainbow—something profane, something sacred, kind of a JB specialty. The rest of the show returned to a bit more SWAG business. Bieber welcomed a series of guests: fellow Coachella performer Dijon for “DEVOTION,” Tems for “I THINK YOU’RE SPECIAL,” and Wizkid for the Tems duet “Essence,” which Bieber joined in on. Finally, guitar whiz kid Mk.gee came onstage to shred subtly for “DAISIES,” bringing the energy up on the exhilarating finale of a surprisingly emotional set. Although he was providing excellent fan service, the choice to do essentially auto-karaoke will be controversial among pop fans who often complain about the wide gap in expected effort between male and female artists. But there was something beautiful, even thrilling, about watching Bieber trawl YouTube—and to be clear, he seemed to be trawling the actual YouTube site, because he complained about slow wifi when “Sorry” took a long time to load—for the massively-popular songs whose candy-sweet production he seems to have left behind, maybe for good. And there was something a little melancholy about it too—especially watching Bieber watch his younger self crash into that glass door. Humiliation for an audience’s amusement is certainly a primary power source for the internet. (One of Bieber’s most compelling career stats is having the most-disliked video on YouTube for eight years in a row.) But to be a fan of Justin Bieber is to have a natural inclination to protect him from harm, even and especially the harm he’s done to himself. He was just a kid, and now he’s a man, and by bringing those old videos into his performance rather than just singing the songs, he seems to want us to remind us all that the transition from childhood to adulthood was just as grueling as it seemed. As the outro to “DAISIES” played, a more triumphant image formed: Bieber, his famous name splashed across the LCD screens, fireworks popping behind him, leaning down toward the kind of camera that has been pointed at him since he was a tween, making heart hands.

title: Justin Bieber Coachella Set Sparks Massive Streaming Surge
description: Justin Bieber's Coachella performance has delivered a significant post-show streaming surge, driving 21 of his songs into Spotify’s Global Top 200.
author: Kennedy French
text: Apr 14, 2026 11:28am PT Justin Bieber’s Coachella Set Sparks Massive Streaming Boost, Sends ‘Baby,’ ‘Daisies’ and More Into Spotify Global Top 200 By Kennedy French Plus Icon Kennedy French Latest ‘The Social Reckoning’ Trailer: Jeremy Strong Is Mark Zuckerberg in Aaron Sorkin’s ‘Social Network’ Sequel 6 hours ago ‘Paradise’ Star Shailene Woodley Created a Quiet Backstory for Annie That Never Made the Cut 3 weeks ago ‘Summer of ’94’ Doc Filmmakers and Soccer Icons Reflect on Coach Bora Milutinovic’s Lasting Legacy 3 weeks ago See All Coachella YouTube Justin Bieber’s Coachella headlining performance is proving the Beliebers right. The performance has delivered a significant post-show streaming surge, driving 21 of his songs into Spotify’s Global Top 200 — the most of any artist following the festival set. According to platform data, Bieber reached No. 1 on Spotify’s Global Top Artist chart following the performance, as his catalog surpassed 77 million streams in a single day, marking his biggest streaming day of the year. The gains were driven by a mix of legacy hits and newer material, including “Beauty and a Beat” at No. 3, “Baby” at No. 12 and “Daisies” at No. 16. On Apple Music, his catalog rose 80% in a single day, also his highest of the year. Related Stories Havas Acquires Culture and Sports Marketing Agency Archrival Greece Selected as the Country of Honor for the Next Edition of the Cannes Film Market “ SWAG ” album consumption in the U.S. is up 80% week-to-date and is pacing toward its biggest week of 2026. The project also recorded its highest streaming day since February, while Bieber placed 13 songs on the Spotify Daily Top Songs U.S. chart, including “Daisies” in the top 25 and “Yukon” in the top 40. Popular on Variety The streaming acceleration follows a widely discussed Coachella set that leaned heavily into minimalism and self-referential nostalgia. Bieber opened the 90-minute performance with 11 tracks from “Swag” and “Swag II” before shifting into a stripped-down, improvisational interlude that unfolded onstage with a laptop and archival clips from his early career. As Variety’s Chris Willman wrote in his review, the show felt like “Personal Time With Justin,” noting its emphasis on mood over spectacle and describing the laptop segment as “the only part of the hour-and-a-half set that brought in any catalog material,” stretching nearly 25 minutes. During that sequence, Bieber revisited early viral and catalog moments including “Baby,” “Favorite Girl,” “That Should Be Me” and “Beauty and a Beat,” alongside non-musical clips such as footage of himself as a child, including video of a young Bieber walking into a glass door. As Willman puts it: “Charm and chops go a long way. Once he showed his face and deigned to actually beam, it was clear that four years of avoiding public live performance have done nothing to put a ding in his charisma.” Bieber is set to headline this Saturday again at the Coachella Main Stage. title: Justin Bieber Dressed Down in a Hoodie for His Coachella Headlining Set | GQ
description: Two months after his shirtlessly vulnerable Grammys triumph, the pop star chose guarded comfort in a Skylrk sweatshirt and shades for his “Bieberchella” return.
author: Eileen Cartter
text: Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story When Justin Bieber stepped onto the Grammys stage in February, for his first solo live performance in years, he laid himself bare . The Canadian pop star emerged shirtless, dressed only in glittery silk boxers and dark socks from his own clothing brand Skylrk , with an electric guitar slung across his tattooed torso. His stark, triumphant performance of “Yukon,” a single from his seventh studio album Swag , was understood to be something of a teaser for his highly anticipated Coachella headlining sets, the first of which took place in Indio, California last night. But for his “Bieberchella” return, a mostly still-lone Bieber instead cocooned himself—in a slouchy zippered red hoodie and black “Speed Racer” shades, also from Skylrk—as he climbed and padded his way around a sparse, abstractly futuristic, Ye-like set in lug-soled Loewe cutout boots. Justin Bieber performing at Coachella 2026 on Saturday night. Kevin Mazur/Getty Images The 32-year-old primarily performed songs off of Swag and its little brother, Swag II , save for a portion midway through his set, when he shed his sweatshirt shroud to do some scrolling on his laptop. Manually typing into YouTube’s search bar projected onto the massive screen behind him, Bieber pulled up his old music videos and footage of his earliest live performances, which he fleetingly sang along to, as well as a few of his past on-stage fails ( whoopsie, ouch! ) and unrelated meme clips ( deez nuts! ). Beneath the hoodie, he wore a raw-hemmed Skylrk fleece tee and below-the-knee waxy black denim shorts by Lu’u Dan . “Alright I’m getting pulled into the deep, dark web,” Bieber said when he finally stepped away from the MacBook. “We gotta keep this show going, man.” He launched into the final section with “Yukon,” segueing into a slew of duets featuring Dijon , Tems, Wizkid , and Mk.gee. In his boxers at the Grammys, Bieber dug himself into an oddly confident groove; his shirtlessless read as both a canny show of vulnerability and a bit of an on-brand shrug. (“I don’t think he decided what the outfit would be until he walked on stage,” Grammys executive producer Ben Winston told Rolling Stone .) Either way, the surprising nerve of his getup, combined with the less-surprising verve of his still-excellent vocals, seemed to move the room full of his industry peers as much as it did the viewers tuned in at home. Midway through his performance, Bieber stripped off his hoodie to reveal a raw-hemmed tee. Kevin Mazur/Getty Images Bieber had a similarly rapt at-home audience for his time on the Coachella stage, on which he dressed and acted more like he has during many of the pseudo-vérité Twitch streams he’s hosted in recent months. He looked cozy, maybe overly so. Online, fans lamented Bieber’s too-relaxed stage presence and lack of Super Bowl halftime show-caliber production, which has (understandably, given the astronomic ticket prices) come to be expected of Coachella headliners—the sort of full-tilt razzle dazzle that Sabrina Carpenter brought to the main stage on Friday. Others framed Bieberchella’s lo-fi, we’re-all-just-hanging-out-here format as a kiss-off metacommentary on the pop star’s career that began on YouTube in 2008, when his former manager Scooter Braun discovered a video of a young Bieber singing on the platform. The “Skylrk Oasis” pop-up at Coachella. Courtesy of Abigail Wornock for Skylrk But Coachella, a gargantuan music festival in its 25th year of existence, is now also the Olympics of influencer-led marketing and brand activations. Bieber and his Skylrk label took full advantage of the opportunity, building out a 10,000-square-foot “Skylrk Oasis” on the festival grounds—the brand’s biggest stateside activation yet. Shaded by temporarily relocated palm trees and cooled by misting stations, the space featured, per a press release, video screens showing “mesmeric” visuals and a pop-up shop stocked with Coachella-exclusive merch: hoodies, skull-cap beanies , and tees printed with slogans like “Biebervelli” and “It’s Not Clocking.” (The latter phrase is a quote from Bieber’s viral confrontation with the paparazzi last year—which he later sampled on Swag and also conjured during his mid-set YouTube-karaoke session.) The drop also featured new colors of the brand’s “Sizzler” silicone iPhone case, which boast a joint-shaped “integrated multi-use holder,” a riff on his wife Hailey Bieber’s viral lip-gloss phone case. (In December, Hailey told GQ that she personally approved Justin’s use of her copyrighted design, saying that she thought the joint-holder case was “really sick and really fucking cool.”) Arrow Arrow Indeed, the entrepreneurial Biebers both rode the Bieberchella economic wave this week. Hailey herself came up with the idea for the best offering of the bunch: a white baby tee printed with the words “Future Mrs. Bieber.” (She posted the T-shirt on her Instagram story with the caption, “Made this for the ones who have a good sense of humor.”) In the week leading up the festival, Hailey and Justin also released a joint capsule collection for her beauty label Rhode that included banana-flavored lip gloss and hydrocolloid zit stickers in novelty shapes such as daisies and mushrooms. (Her brand also hosted its own Instagrammable Coachella pop-up shop, as it has for the last several years.) Bieber’s official Skylrk-branded Coachella merch also included more tees featuring his viral phrase, plus a mesh jersey printed with an image of a two-middle-fingers-up Justin ( as worn by Kylie Jenner , a friend of the Biebers) and a tee of a bikini-clad Hailey. The official Justin Bieber merch for sale at Coachella featured photos of Justin, as well as his wife Hailey. VALERIE MACON/Getty Images Per Skylrk, on-the-ground demand for Bieberchella merch has been so high that the site-specific goods are now available for preorder on the brand’s website . The label also claims that, as of Sunday morning, its on-site sales are on track to surpass all-time Coachella records for merch sales. As promised by the text already printed on the commemorative hoodies, Bieber is expected back next Saturday for Coachella weekend two.

title: Justin Bieber's Coachella Performance And Setlist
description: I was NOT ready for this iconic performance.
author: Sara De Ledesma
text: by Sara De Ledesma BuzzFeed BuzzFeed Staff As a full-time writer at BuzzFeed Canada, I cover all things pop culture and food. Table of Contents The SWAG Era The Justice Era The My World Era The Believe Era The Journals Era The Pre-Fame Era The Purpose Era When Justin Bieber hit the stage at Coachella, people didn't really know what to expect. Full-on choreography? An all-purple outfit? A dramatic "One Less Lonely Girl" moment? Instead, what we got was something way more unexpected — and honestly, way more Justin . Kevin Mazur / Getty Images / Coachella There were no flashy theatrics and no overproduced spectacle. Just Bieber walking out and bringing a performance that was intentionally laid-back, slightly chaotic, and deeper than you think. Justin wasn't here to prove himself, but to celebrate an iconic career that doesn't plan on stopping anytime soon. So in celebration of his performance (and in preparation for weekend 2👀), here's every era that Justin Bieber took us through: The SWAG Era Kevin Mazur / Getty Images / Coachella Kicking off with a long stretch of newer material from his latest album, SWAG, Bieber stepped onto a circular stage and sang along to tracks like "ALL I CAN TAKE," "SPEED DEMON," and "FIRST PLACE." If you were expecting a big flashy opener, this was quite the opposite. This era feels like Bieber is fully in his "I don't need to prove anything" vibe. It's stripped back, vibey, and not very concerned about being a hit-maker. The Justice Era Kevin Mazur / Getty Images / Coachella Before shifting gears, Bieber brought out surprise guest, The Kid LAROI to perform their massive hit "STAY" together. Amping up the crowd, people were seen screaming, jumping, and fully losing it. It was a quick moment, but it did exactly what it needed to do: remind everyone that Justin can still drop a massive pop moment and do it very effortlessly. The My World Era Coachella And then, the entire set flipped. Bieber sat down, went onto a laptop, and pulled up YouTube — the very platform that made him famous. Starting off with "Baby," he played his old music videos and sang along karaoke-style, layering his deeper voice over his childhood vocals. I'm sorry, who greenlit this emotional attack on my heart?! Alongside songs from that album, he followed that up with "Favorite Girl," "That Should Be Me," and "Never Say Never." This was when the signature purple outfit graced the stage countless times. If you don't know this era, can you really call yourself a Belieber? It was all about the flashy choreo, the over-the-top theatrics, and Justin pulled it back by simply singing along to those days. And honestly? I'm so here for it. Mark Von Holden / WireImage The Believe Era Coachella After that emotional throwback, Bieber transitioned into the Believe era with "Beauty and a Beat." This was when he transitioned from teen idol into a full-on pop star. Even though this was the only song Bieber pulled out from this album, it was undeniably an iconic one. The Journals Era Coachella Without missing a beat, Bieber slid into the Journals era with songs like "Confident" and "All That Matters." A smoother, more R&B heavy, and effortlessly cool sound, these songs stood out for how different they feel from the rest of his catalogue. This is one of his most underrated albums, and honestly felt like a reward for the true fans (I screamed when I saw this on the live stream). The Pre-Fame Era Coachella Just when you thought the nostalgia was over, we were just getting started. Bieber took things even further back by pulling up his old YouTube covers of "With You" by Chris Brown and "So Sick" by Ne-Yo . We're talking grainy video, terrible lighting, and little Bieber. Him singing to his younger self? I'm gonna cry. The Purpose Era Then came the Purpose era with "Sorry" and "Where Are Ü Now." These were the more EDM-inspired and high energy songs of his discography. This was one of Bieber's biggest comebacks — you simply couldn't escape these songs even if you tried. Kevin Mazur / Getty Images / Coachella To close out the set, Bieber returned to his newer sound — except this time, with guests. He brought out Dijon for "DEVOTION," and Tems and WizKid for "Essence." These performances felt loose, organic, and almost more jam-session like. So, all in all, instead of giving fans a straightforward, chronological performance, Bieber took us all over the board. There were no big speeches, just raw vocals, talent, and vibes. Honestly, I loved it. There's something special about seeing him unpolished yet self-aware, and just comfortable being back on stage. So, what did you think of Justin Bieber's performance? Do you think he'll switch anything up for weekend 2? Let us know all your thoughts in the comments! Kevin Mazur / Getty Images / Coachella For more of the latest, be sure to follow BuzzFeed Canada on TikTok and Instagram ! Comments
